attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
==== ISSUE 145 ====
TITLE: Unquoted $IGNORES expansion word-splits and glob-expands allowlist lines
SEV: LOW
FILE: .github/workflows/ci.yml
LINE: 92-95
DESC: `IGNORES` is accumulated as a single string and passed unquoted: `pip-audit ... $IGNORES`. Real advisory IDs (GHSA-*, PYSEC-*, CVE-*) are word-split-safe, so today it works for valid input — but the ci.yml comment invites "one advisory id per line" without forbidding comments, and any line containing spaces (e.g. an accidental `# comment`) is split into multiple bogus `--ignore-vuln` tokens. I reproduced: a `# a comment line` line becomes `--ignore-vuln # a comment line` (4 tokens). pip-audit happens to accept unknown ignore IDs without erroring (I confirmed `--ignore-vuln '# a comment line'` exits 0), so it fails open silently rather than loudly. Combined with the unquoted expansion, a stray glob char in the file could also undergo pathname expansion. This is fragile, fail-open input handling in the security gate.
FIX: Build an array instead of a string: `args=(); while ...; do args+=(--ignore-vuln "$id"); done` then `pip-audit "${args[@]}"`. Strip comments/whitespace and validate each ID against an expected pattern (e.g. `^(GHSA|PYSEC|CVE)-`).

==== ISSUE 146 ====
TITLE: Over-wide version range for arize-phoenix (>=7.0,<18) admits 10 major versions
SEV: LOW
FILE: pyproject.toml
LINE: 55
DESC: `arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18` spans eleven major versions (7 through 17). The installed/resolved version is 17.6.0, ten majors above the floor. A range this wide defeats the point of the cap: any breaking change or a freshly-disclosed advisory in any major 7-17 is admitted by a clean install, and the engine has no lockfile to constrain it (see lockfile finding). Other extras are reasonably bounded (single-major caps), making this one an outlier. Same family is also slightly inconsistent: langgraph>=1.2,<2 is duplicated across the `persistence` and `orchestration` extras (functionally fine, but two sources of truth for one bound).
FIX: Tighten the phoenix cap to the majors actually tested (e.g. `>=17,<18`, matching the resolved 17.6.0) or whatever range integration CI truly exercises, and de-duplicate the langgraph bound (define it once and reference it) to avoid drift.

==== ISSUE 147 ====
TITLE: No SBOM emission or automated dependency/license/transitive-monitoring in CI
SEV: LOW
FILE: .github/workflows/ci.yml
LINE: 78-95
DESC: For a production security/privacy engine, the supply-chain story is point-in-time advisory scanning only. There is no SBOM artifact (pip-audit supports `-f cyclonedx-json`/`cyclonedx-xml` and cyclonedx-python-lib is already pulled in transitively), no `.github/dependabot.yml` (no automated transitive-update/PR monitoring), and no automated license check. I scanned all 230 installed dists for strong copyleft: the only two 'gpl' substring hits were false positives (matplotlib = PSF, pandas = BSD-3-Clause), so the license posture is currently clean — but nothing in CI would catch a future transitive dep introducing a GPL/AGPL component into this Apache-2.0 project, or a newly-disclosed CVE between PRs.
FIX: Emit and archive an SBOM in the audit job (`pip-audit -f cyclonedx-json -o sbom.json` as a CI artifact), add a `.github/dependabot.yml` (pip ecosystem) for transitive-update PRs, and add a lightweight license allowlist check (e.g. pip-licenses or cyclonedx license gate) so copyleft creep fails CI.

1	name: ci
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	
8	jobs:
9	  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
10	  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
11	  core:
12	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
13	    strategy:
14	      matrix:
15	        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
16	    steps:
17	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
18	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
19	        with:
20	          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
21	      - name: Install (no extras)
22	        run: |
23	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
24	          pip install -e ".[dev]"
25	      - name: Lint (ruff)
26	        run: ruff check src tests
27	      - name: Format check (ruff)
28	        run: ruff format --check src tests
29	      - name: Type-check (mypy)
30	        run: mypy
31	      - name: Test (pytest)
32	        run: pytest -q
33	
34	  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
35	  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
36	  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
37	  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
38	  # e2e) run for real.
39	  integration:
40	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
41	    services:
42	      postgres:
43	        image: postgres:16
44	        env:
45	          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
46	          POSTGRES_DB: assay
47	        ports: ["5432:5432"]
48	        options: >-
49	          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
50	          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
51	      qdrant:
52	        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
53	        ports: ["6333:6333"]
54	    env:
55	      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
56	      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/assay
57	    steps:
58	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
59	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
60	        with:
61	          python-version: "3.12"
62	      - name: Install (all extras)
63	        run: |
64	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
65	          pip install -e ".[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]"
66	      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
67	        run: mypy
68	      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
69	      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
70	      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
71	      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
72	      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
73	        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing
74	
75	  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
76	  # with advisories). The scan runs against the committed, fully-pinned, hashed lockfile
77	  # (requirements.lock) so the audit is reproducible across runs and matches what installs
78	  # (#108, #130, #131, #132, #145, #146). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id
79	  # per line, '#' comments and blank lines allowed) covers accepted/unfixable ones.
80	  audit:
81	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
82	    steps:
83	      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
84	      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
85	        with:
86	          python-version: "3.12"
87	      - name: Install pip-audit
88	        run: python -m pip install --upgrade pip pip-audit
89	      - name: Lockfile is in sync with pyproject (reproducibility guard)
90	        run: |
91	          python -m pip install --upgrade uv
92	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock
93	          # ignore the auto-generated header (timestamp/command line) when diffing
94	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements.regen.lock) \
95	            || { echo "::error::requirements.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements.lock"; exit 1; }
96	      - name: pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)
97	        run: |
98	          set -euo pipefail
99	          # --strict: any dependency that cannot be collected/audited FAILS the gate (no silent
100	          # skips). --require-hashes: every pin must carry a hash. Build --ignore-vuln args as an
101	          # array so allowlist entries can't word-split or glob-expand, and read the file
102	          # newline-agnostically so the last line is honored even without a trailing newline.
103	          IGNORES=()
104	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
105	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
106	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
107	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
108	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
109	          fi
110	          pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"
111	      - name: SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile
112	        run: |
113	          set -euo pipefail
114	          # Emit a machine-readable software bill of materials for the pinned dependency set so
115	          # downstream license/transitive monitoring has a stable artifact to track (#147).
116	          pip-audit --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json || true
117	          test -s sbom.cyclonedx.json && echo "SBOM emitted ($(wc -c < sbom.cyclonedx.json) bytes)"
118	      - name: Upload SBOM artifact
